channel.title: Dialectics on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Dialectics on emsenn.net

item.title: Contradiction
item.description:
In formal logic, contradiction typically means that two claims cannot be true together. In dialectical analysis, contradiction also names a structured tension internal to a form, where the conditions of that form’s reproduction generate forces that destabilize it.

Dialectical contradiction is not a permission to ignore rigor. It is a methodological signal: identify how incompatibility is produced, mediated, and historically transformed.

¶Related terms

  • Dialectics
  • Mediation
  • Historicization

item.title: Contradiction and Mediation in Dialectical Analysis
item.description:
Dialectical writing often names contradiction but skips mediation. That skip is the main reason many analyses sound dialectical while remaining methodologically thin.

A contradiction, by itself, is an incompatibility claim. In strong analysis, the contradiction must be shown as internal to the object’s own conditions of reproduction. Then mediation must be traced: institutions, practices, temporal phases, and relation chains that carry the tension into transformation.

This two-part requirement appears in different traditions with different vocabulary. In Hegelian framing, contradiction and mediation are internal moments of conceptual development [@hegel-dialectics-sep-2019]. In Maoist strategic analysis, contradiction must be differentiated and located concretely (principal/secondary, aspect shifts) rather than handled abstractly [@mao-on-contradiction-1937].

item.title: Determinate Negation
item.description:
Determinate negation is negation with content. A form is not merely rejected; it is transformed according to its own internal limits, producing a successor form that preserves certain determinations while reconfiguring their role [@hegel-dialectics-sep-2019].

This distinguishes determinate negation from abstract negation:

  • Abstract negation: simple opposition (not-X).
  • Determinate negation: structured transition (X becomes re-specified through its contradiction).

The concept explains how dialectical development can involve real rupture without becoming arbitrary. What follows is constrained by what is negated.

item.title: Dialectics Reference Sheet
item.description:
This is a reference text in the Diataxis sense. Use it as a compact lookup when reading or writing dialectical analysis.

¶Core terms

  • Dialectics: analysis of development through internal contradiction.
  • Determinate Negation: transformation specific to a form’s own limits.
  • Immanence: explanation from internal relations rather than external transcendence.
  • Mediation: intermediate mechanisms that connect one determination to another.
  • Historicization: treatment of forms as historically produced and therefore transformable.
  • Endoxa: reputable starting opinions in dialectical testing.

¶Minimal diagnostic checklist

A strong dialectical claim should answer all six:

item.title: Endoxa
item.description:
Endoxa are reputable opinions: propositions accepted by all, by most, or by recognized authorities. In Aristotelian dialectic, they provide starting points for inquiry, not final guarantees of truth [@aristotle-logic-sep].

The methodological role of endoxa is twofold:

  1. They provide shared premises that make argument possible.
  2. They provide targets for criticism, refinement, or rejection when contradictions or counter-cases appear.

Using endoxa means beginning from historically available judgment while keeping those judgments revisable through argument.

item.title: Genealogy of Dialectics
item.description:
¶Orientation

A genealogy asks not “what is the final definition?” but “through what transformations did this practice become what it is now?” A genealogy of dialectics therefore tracks shifts in function, not only shifts in vocabulary.

The word “dialectic” persists across millennia, but its role changes:

  • from conversational testing,
  • to logical training,
  • to a theory of conceptual development,
  • to a method for analyzing historical-material contradiction.

The continuity is the concern with contradiction and revision. The breaks are about where contradiction lives and what dialectics is for.

item.title: Historicization
item.description:
Historicization is the method of treating an object as historically produced rather than timelessly given.

To historicize a concept is to ask when it emerged, through which conflicts or practices it stabilized, and how its meaning shifts across periods. To historicize an institution is to track the sequence of forces, decisions, and constraints through which it was formed and reorganized.

In dialectical work, historicization prevents abstraction from freezing social forms into essences. It keeps analysis tied to determinate conditions and temporal development.

item.title: History of Dialectics
item.description:
¶Scope

If the genealogy asks how dialectics came to be, this history asks how dialectics has been historically used, reworked, institutionalized, and contested.

The focus here is modern and contemporary circulation: from Hegel’s system, through Marxist transformation, into twentieth-century party, critical-theoretical, and anti-colonial formations.

¶1) Nineteenth century pivot: from speculative system to social critique

Hegel’s mature work made dialectics a comprehensive philosophical method for conceptual development [@hegel-dialectics-sep-2019]. By the later nineteenth century, Marx’s intervention shifted the center of gravity. Marx’s own formulation in the 1873 afterword is explicit: he inverts Hegel’s method by grounding movement in material social relations [@marx-capital-afterword-1873].

item.title: How to Evaluate a Dialectical Argument
item.description:
This is a how-to text. It gives a practical procedure for evaluating a specific argument.

¶Goal

Determine whether an argument uses dialectics as method, not only as style.

¶Steps

¶1) Isolate the object

Write one sentence naming the object under analysis (institution, concept, social relation, historical process).

If the object is unclear, stop. No dialectical evaluation is possible.

item.title: Immanence
item.description:
Immanence is the claim that causes, structures, and standards of critique are internal to the forms being analyzed.

In dialectical method, immanent critique evaluates a position using that position’s own claims and commitments, showing how internal tensions produce revision or breakdown.

In metaphysics, immanence is often contrasted with transcendence: explanation is sought within being, relation, or process itself, not in an external principle standing outside the world.

¶Related terms

  • Ontology
  • Metaphysics
  • Dialectics
  • Mediation

item.title: Immanent Critique
item.description:
Immanent critique is criticism from within: it evaluates practices, institutions, or theories using norms they themselves claim to uphold. In critical theory, this method is central because it links diagnosis to historically situated contradictions rather than external ideal models [@critical-theory-sep-2023].

Immanent critique differs from merely internal critique. It is not only consistency checking; it is transformative analysis of crisis-tendencies within a form of life.

¶Related terms

  • Immanence
  • Contradiction
  • Historicization

item.title: Mediation
item.description:
Mediation is the principle that determinations do not stand alone or move in isolation. They are connected through intermediate relations, processes, and forms.

In dialectics, mediation opposes immediacy. An immediate account says A produces B directly. A mediated account asks which structures, temporal phases, and relation chains connect A to B.

In social analysis, mediation helps distinguish structural explanation from narrative sequence. Saying “crisis produced reform” is incomplete unless the institutions, conflicts, and mechanisms that carried the transition are specified.

item.title: Nonidentity
item.description:
Nonidentity names the mismatch between concept and object: objects are never exhausted by the concepts that classify them. In Adorno’s negative dialectics, this is not a minor epistemic inconvenience but a structural condition for critique [@adorno-sep-2025].

Nonidentity blocks premature reconciliation. It keeps thought attentive to what identity-thinking suppresses: particularity, remainder, and the historical violence embedded in forced conceptual sameness.

¶Related terms

  • Dialectics
  • Reification
  • Totality

item.title: Nonidentity and Negative Dialectics
item.description:
Negative dialectics refuses the claim that contradiction is resolved into a final conceptual reconciliation. Its core safeguard is nonidentity: objects are not fully identical to the concepts that organize them [@adorno-sep-2025].

This matters methodologically. Without nonidentity, dialectical analysis can become system-confirming: every conflict is narrated as a moment on the way to closure. With nonidentity, critique remains open to remainder, injury, and historical specificity that concepts fail to absorb.

In practical terms, nonidentity is a discipline against premature synthesis. It requires analysts to track what their conceptual scheme cannot capture without distortion.

item.title: Principal and Secondary Contradiction: Use Cases
item.description:
Mao’s distinction between principal and secondary contradiction is most useful when handled as a stage-specific analytic tool, not as a rigid formula [@mao-on-contradiction-1937].

¶Use case 1: Policy conflict analysis

When multiple policy conflicts coexist, the method asks which contradiction structures the overall field at that stage. Secondary contradictions still matter, but they are interpreted through the principal one.

¶Use case 2: Movement strategy

In movement situations, principal contradiction identifies strategic priority while secondary contradictions identify alliance constraints, internal tensions, and tactical sequencing.

item.title: Principal Contradiction
item.description:
Principal contradiction identifies which contradiction is decisive in a conjuncture when multiple contradictions coexist. In Mao’s formulation, this role can shift as conditions change; principal status is historical, not eternal [@mao-on-contradiction-1937].

This concept is strategic: it orders analytic and political priority.

¶Related terms

  • Secondary Contradiction
  • Contradiction
  • Historicization

item.title: Reification
item.description:
Reification is the transformation of historically produced social relations into apparently natural or thing-like facts.

In Marxist and Lukácsian analysis, reification obscures mediation: what is socially produced appears as if it were simply given, autonomous, and beyond alteration [@lukacs-sep-2023].

The critical task is de-reification: recovering the processes, relations, and institutions that produce the appearance of objectivity.

¶Related terms

  • Totality
  • Dialectics
  • Historicization

item.title: Reification and Totality in Modern Critical Theory
item.description:
In twentieth-century critical theory, totality and reification form a paired diagnostic framework. Reification names how social relations appear as thing-like facts; totality names the method required to undo that appearance by reconnecting parts to the structured whole [@lukacs-sep-2023].

The pairing is methodological, not decorative. If reification is named without totality, critique stops at denunciation. If totality is named without reification, analysis risks abstract system-talk detached from lived forms of domination.

¶Lukácsian contribution

Lukács’s contribution is to make this pairing central to social intelligibility under capitalism: fragmented appearances are not merely epistemic mistakes but socially produced forms of experience [@lukacs-sep-2023].

item.title: Secondary Contradiction
item.description:
Secondary contradictions are contradictions within a process that do not occupy the decisive position at a given stage. They can intensify, recede, and under changed conditions become principal [@mao-on-contradiction-1937].

Treating secondary contradictions as irrelevant is an error; the concept marks subordination, not insignificance.

¶Related terms

  • Principal Contradiction
  • Contradiction

item.title: Sublation
item.description:
Sublation (German: Aufhebung) names the dialectical movement in which something is negated without being simply destroyed. A prior form is cancelled as self-sufficient, preserved in transformed form, and raised into a richer determination [@hegel-dialectics-sep-2019].

Sublation is a key reason dialectical development is neither pure continuity nor pure rupture.

¶Related terms

  • Determinate Negation
  • Mediation
  • Dialectics

item.title: Sublation Beyond the Triad
item.description:
The “thesis-antithesis-synthesis” formula remains pedagogically popular, but it often obscures what sublation actually names. Sublation is not a third-step compromise. It is the structured movement by which a form is negated, preserved, and transformed [@hegel-dialectics-sep-2019].

¶Why the triad fails

The triad model encourages template thinking: find two opposites, then announce a synthesis. This can produce elegant narratives with weak method. Sublation, by contrast, requires determinate transition.

item.title: Totality
item.description:
Totality is the methodological demand to analyze parts through the structured whole that conditions their meaning and function.

In dialectical usage, totality does not mean a closed or final system. It means that phenomena are relationally constituted: labor, law, state, and culture are not self-explanatory in isolation.

In Lukácsian terms, totality is central to overcoming fragmented or reified appearances in social analysis [@lukacs-sep-2023].

¶Related terms

  • Dialectics
  • Mediation
  • Historicization

item.title: Tutorial: Tracing Dialectical Development
item.description:
This is a tutorial text. It teaches one beginner workflow through a small, guided exercise.

¶Prerequisites

  • Dialectics
  • Determinate Negation
  • Mediation
  • Historicization

¶Learning objective

By the end, you should be able to produce a five-line dialectical map of a short argument.

¶Step 1: Start with a tiny claim

Use this toy claim:

“A labor market that treats labor as a commodity depends on stable workers, yet competitive pressure destabilizes those workers’ lives.”

item.title: Uneven and Combined Development
item.description:
Uneven and combined development names a historical pattern in which different developmental tempos coexist and interpenetrate, producing hybrid social forms rather than linear stage succession [@trotsky-hrr-1930].

In contemporary theory, it functions as a general historical-dialectical category for relational development under asymmetry [@rosenberg-ucd-2013].

¶Related terms

  • Historicization
  • Totality
  • History of Dialectics
